meaning: 'and, but, then' (see Dillmann 1907:521ff); wa-hi, wa-ni 'and also, further'; wa-ssa 'but as' (e.g. wa-əmməssa 'but if');
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. wa 'and', Sar.Ph.Ug. w, Heb.Aram. Syr. wə, Akk. u. ETHIOPIC: Te. wā, Har. -wā, Gur. -wā, Amh. wā (from Geez); also in Cushitic: Be. wā 'and'. The derivation of Har. -wā from Ar. wa, as suggested by Littmann, ZA 33 (1921). 107, is doubtful. For the usage of wə, wə in Semitic, see Brockelmann 1913: 462f.

meaning: 'where, in the place, along'; ba-wa-əda, ba-wəda 'by the side of'; haba wəda 'at'; mangala wəda 'by the side of'; əmwəda, əmənna wəda 'from where'; bo wəda 'somewhere'.
origin_explanation: possibly from the root for 'hand' (see əd) with a deictic w (so also Eitan, AJSL 46[1929-30].47). For the parts of the body used as prepositions, see Brockelmann 1913:421ff. SEMITIC: Soq. 'id 'toward', Hars. wede. ETHIOPIC: Amh. wədä 'toward'.

meaning: 'he, this, that, that same, such, that one, the, he himself, he is'; also used as copula with the 1st and 2nd persons; e.g. 'ana wa-ətu 'I am', 'ənta wa-ətu 'you are'; za-wa-ətu 'that is to say, namely'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. huwa, Ar. (Dat) hū, Sar. h, Mh. he, Sb. še, Heb. hū (hī), Aram. h, hū, hū(ʔ), Syr. hū, Ph. h, Ug. hw, Akk. šū. For the pronoun augmented by t, cp. Ar. (Spain) huet, Palestinian Ar. hūtu, SAR hwt, Mh. huti, Ph. hmt (pl), Ug. hwt, Akk. šuātti. (gen.-acc.). Brockelmann 1908:241, 303 derives G. wa-ətu from *hū-ətu > *ū-ətu, *wū-ətu > wa-ətu. Rundgren 1955:188 expresses doubts concerning this derivation, and on p. 194 he suggests the possibility of *hū-hī-ti > *hū-hī-tū > *hə-ə-tū > wa-ətu. For the 3rd person, masc. fem. 'he, she', see Barth 1913:14-18. See also -tu. ETHIOPIC: Te. hotu, Gaf. wət; final -t also in Gur. xut, xuta, ul. Rundgren 1955:189 compares the Tna. demonstrative 'ətu 'this'. For other Ethiopian languages, see Leslau 1956:54. See also ya-əti, wa-ətomu, wa-əton.

from wa-ə- and the suffix pronoun of the 3rd pers. plural -omu. For the 3rd pers. pl. masc. fem., see Barth 1913:18-22. ETHIOPIC: Te. hətəm. For the other Ethiopian languages, see Leslau 1956:54. See also wa-əton.
from wa-ət- (taken from the masculine) and the suffix pronoun of the 3rd. fem. pl. -on. See wa-ətomu, above.

meaning: 'pass the day, remain, stay, do something during the day'; wa-əla 'cause to pass the day, place in custody'; wa-əli 'guard, retainer, attendant, servant, partisan, official, executioner, soldier'; wa-əal 'one who spends the day'; wa-əalo, in gabra wa-əalo 'he spent the day resting'.

(pl. əlatāt) 'day, time, space of time, (Lt) 'fate'; (əlat in a construct state position means 'daily'; e.g. sisəya əlat 'daily food'); (əlatta + day of the week expresses the day of the week; e.g. əlatta ərb 'Friday'); (əlatta + verb, əma əlatta + verb 'when'); (ba-əlatu 'the very day'); (ya-əata əlatta 'then'); (zətti əlat 'today'); (za-əalla əlat 'daily'); (əmwəlat əlat, əmwəlat la-əlat 'day-by-day'); əwə-əli, məwə-əli (K*), act. part. of əwə-əla; mə-əlt, mə-əlt (pl. məwə-əl, məwə-əlāt) 'day, daytime, noontime'; (mə-əltä 'during the day'); məwə-əl, məwə-əlāt (pl.) 'period, era, time'; məwə-əl, məwə-əl 'place of custody, place of detention, prison'; mə-əltāwi 'daily, midday (adj)'; məwə-ələnna, in baluya məwə-ələnna 'eternity'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Brockelmann 1908:237 (following Praetorius) connects w-ə-l with w-d (Ar. wa-əda 'promise'), with r resulting from dissimilation in a form such as *maw-ədt > mə-əlt, and l becoming generalized in the root. The suggestion made by Bravmann, ZS 9 (1933).153-4 to connect w-l with Akk. a-əlu 'bind, conclude a contract' is not convincing. ETHIOPIC: Tna. wə-əla 'spend the day', Te. wə-əla (also əla, for which see Cohen 1924:131), Amh. Gaf.Gur. wəla, Arg. wəla, Har. wəla, wawala. For mə-əlt 'day', cp. Te. mə-əal, Tna. mə-əlti, Har. mə-əltu, mältu, Gur. m-əltu, mält. The root also occurs in Cushitic: Or.Som. əla, ol 'spend the day'; perhaps also Bil. wān, Kham. wən (with alternance of liquids). See also ma-əla II.
SEMITIC: Ar. wa-əal, wa-əil 'mountain goat', Sar. w-əl, Mh. wāl, Heb. ya-əel, Aram.-Syr. ya-əla, Ug. y-əl. ETHIOPIC: Amh. wə-əla 'mountain goat' (taken from Geez); also in Cushitic: Qua. wəla.

meaning: 'be rough, be coarse, (K.T.Y) be amazing, be amazed, be overawed, (KG) change, transform'; (the Amharic rendering of wə-əra with tädämmäqä, tädämmämä given by Tayyä means 'be stupefied' rather than 'admire', as given by M 442);
origin_explanation: əwə-əra (K*), causative; təwə-əra, təwə-əra 'be stupefied, not understand, look at with astonishment' (transferred meanings); wə-əri (K*), act. part. of wə-əra; wə-ə(ə)r 'difficult to understand, unheard of, paradox, paradoxical, (T) amazing, (K) different, (KG) change' (n); wə-əur (K), same; wə-əri (Lt) 'paradox, incomprehensible matter, (T) amazement'. SEMITIC: Ar. wa-əara 'be rugged, uneven', Ar. (Yemen) wə-əra 'thicket', to be compared with Heb. ya-əar 'forest', Aram.-Syr. ya-əra, Ph.Ug. y-r, contrary to Leslau 1958:24, and to Guillaume IV:7, who compares Heb. ya-əar 'forest' with Ar. wə-əra 'thicket'. See also wə-əy.

meaning: 'burn (intr), burn up, be burnt, be consumed by fire, be scorched, be on fire, blaze'; əwə-əya 'burn (tr), burn up (tr), scorch, consume (fire), set on fire, give heat'; əstəwə-əya 'kindle, set on fire'; wə-əyi, act. part. of wə-əya; wə-əy (fem. wə-əit) 'hot, burning, burnt, fervent'; wə-əy 'fervor'; wə-ə(ə)y 'heat, burning heat, conflagration'; əy-əat 'burning (n), inflammation, brand mark'; wə-əyat 'burning (n), a burn, inflammation, blaze, conflagration, fervor'; məw-əy, məw-əi, məw-əyi, (Lt) əwə-əyi, part. of əwə-əya; məw-əy (K*) 'place of burning'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: the connection with Heb. yə-əa (y-y) 'carry off' suggested by Ludolf 1899 is unlikely; perhaps Ar. wə-əa (w-y) 'stir up a riot'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. wə-əyä 'be hot', Te. wə-əy 'hot spring', Amh. wə-əy 'heat' (from Geez), Har. wəy 'hot', Gur. wə-əy 'hot'; also in Cushitic: Qua. wə-ə 'burn, be hot'. Or ə-əa 'hot', contrary to Praetorius 1894:323 who derives the Semitic-Ethiopic root from Cushitic.
From Ar. wabā 'infectious disease'; also in Amh. wabā. waba 'disease, malaria'. Gur. woba 'fever, malaria'. Har. woba 'drone (which causes malaria)'; also in Cushitic. Or. woba.
from Amh. waba, same.
from Amh. wābbo 'tāmmane 'leopard'; of Cushitic origin: Sa. wābbo 'wild animal of the cat family'.
None
variant of aboli, oboli, above.
None

meaning: 'finish, complete, bring to a conclusion, make an end, consume'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Sar. d- 'already' (W. Müller 1983:278). ETHIOPIC: Tna. waddəyə 'finish'; also in Cushitic: Aw. widd-ə 'a 'finish'.

meaning: 'put into, join together, insert, (T.M) lay a foundation, establish firmly'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. mawdad (L), mawdād, modād (K), pl. mawāded (D) 'hook, clasp'; is undoubtedly to be identified with the root wdd 'love' developed into 'agree, fit together', as, for instance, in Amh. tāwaddādä 'be joined, fitted together', Tna. tāwaddādä 'join together (intr), concur'. SEMITIC: Ar. wadda 'love', Sar. s₁t-wdd 'consent, agree', m-wd 'friend (of ruler)', Heb. yādīd in yādīd 'beloved', Syr. yaddad 'love', Ug. ydd 'beloved'. Holma 152, n. 2 identifies it with Ar. waṭṭaḍa 'make firm, stabilize'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. waddādä 'love', Arg. waddādä, Har. waddādä, Gur. waddādä; Tna. tāwaddādä 'agree with one another, join together (intr), concur', Amh. tāwaddādä 'be joined, fitted together'.

meaning: (T) wadifot 'rag, scrap, used garment, (T) palm leaf, mat'; (Gr.436) wadufat is probably a misprint for wadifat.
origin_explanation: None

meaning: 'fall, fall down, fall off, be deposed, fall in, collapse, go to ruin, sink, become decayed, throw oneself (into)'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. wadaqa 'fall in drops', Sar. wdq 'fall'. ETHIOPIC: Te. wadaqa 'fall'. Tna. waddāqa, Amh. waddāqa, Arg. waddāqa, Har. waddāqa, Gur. waddāqa; passed into Cushitic: Af. wadqa 'fall'.

SEMITIC: Brockelmann 1927:28 compares it with wdy (Heb. in hōdā 'praise', Ar. 'istawdā) and suggests that s of waddasa is due to 'Reimwortbildung' with qaddasa 'sanctify'. ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. waddāsä 'praise', Te. waddāsa. The derivation from Cushitic: Bil.Sa.Af. waṭā 'singer', Or. wēt, suggested by Praetorius, ZDMG 43 (1889).324-5, is unlikely.
None

meaning: 'put, put in, add, put on (adornments), put under, lay, place, set, throw, cast'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. 'awdā (from wdy) 'take away (death), destroy', Sar. (Qat) m-wdy-n 'cistern' (W. Müller), Heb. yādā (ydy) 'throw', Aram. 'ādī, yādī, Ug. ydy (de Moor 243). Barth 1902:12 connects it with the root wdy 'confess' (as in Heb. hiṭwaddā, Syr. 'awdi, Ar. 'istawdā) whose original meaning would be 'accuse oneself' > 'confess' (so also Koehler 363-4, but not Baumgartner 372 who separates Heb. ydy 1 'throw' from ydy II 'confess'). As for G. 'astawādä 'daya 'accuse, slander' from wdy 'throw', cp. Ar. qadafa 'throw, accuse'. ETHIOPIC: Te. wada (wdy) 'put, make'. Tna. waddāyä 'put'. Praetorius 1879:7 connects G. waddāyä with Amh. (also Gur) waqqā 'acquire, shop'. While G. waddāyä could explain the form of Amh.Gur. waqqā, it could not explain the development of the meanings; it is rather a denominative from waqa 'price' (so also Cohen 1939:228); cp. Tna. (tā)waqaya 'agree on a price'. Amh. wāgawaddāyä 'mention a price repeatedly'. For G. mudāy, cp. Tna.Amh. Gal.Gur. mudāy 'basket', Har. mudāy.

meaning: (T.M) 'wall of stones and mud, house built of stones'
origin_explanation: None

meaning: 'go out, go forth, come out, come forth, depart, exceed, emerge, leave, pass away, escape, dissent, differ (əm), spring from, break out, rise (sun), originate in, be spread (news)'
origin_explanation: None

phonetics: (Lt) modā 'offering, gift, gift to one who is going on a journey';
meaning: 'offering, gift, gift to one who is going on a journey'; mudāʾ 'place of exit, going out, way to escape, source, end'; modāʾt, mudāʾt 'exit, boundary, end'; dā-dā, see above
origin explanation: SEMITIC: Sar. wadf 'go out' Soq. wada 'grow (herbs)', Heb. yāṣā (yṣʾ) 'go out', Aram. yṣʾ 'burst forth, bloom', Syr. (y)yṣʾ 'sprout, spring, come up', Md. yṣʾ, Ug.Ph. yṣʾ, Akk. (w)aṣū 'Noeldeke, ZDMG 40 (1886), 725 compares Ar. dwḥw 'offspring which he derives from a root wd' 'go out' and identifies it with Heb. yṣʾšm'im 'offspring', likewise from wṣʾ 'go out'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. wāṣa 'go out' (from wṣʾ), Amh.Arg. wāṭṭa, Gur. wāṭa. Note that the Geez root wdʾ, wṣʾ means only 'go out', not also 'go up' as does Amh.Arg.Gur. wāṭ(ṭ)a. For the meaning of G. 'as- tawḍaʾu 'spend, contribute', cp. Amh. awāṭṭa 'spend', Amh. awwaṭṭa 'contribute (money)'; also Akk. šīṭu 'expenditure' from waṣā 'go out', Heb. hōṣī (ʾ) késef 'spend'.

meaning: 'hurl by a sling'; tawāddaʾfa 'be hit by a sling, be hurled by a sling'; tawāddaʾfa (K*) 'hit one another by a sling'; wādafi (pl. wadāfəyān, wadaft) 'one who hurls by a sling'; wāduf (K*) 'hurled with a sling'; wāṣdəfta mabraq (Lt) 'flash of lightning'; modaf, modāf, mudāf, modaft (pl. mawādāft) (Lt) moṣaf 'sling, catapult, (TS.Sw. in D 948) stone hurled by a sling'; (sab-a modaf 'slingers')
origin explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. wadafa 'throw with a sling' (considered a Geez loanword by Noeldeke 1910:55, but not so by Landberg 1942:2926), Ar. (Yemen) waṭaf. Akk. (w)aṣpu 'sling'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. wāččāfa 'hurl with a sling'. For modaf 'sling', cp. Tna.Amh.Gur. wānčaf, Gaf. wānčafā. Har wōṭafa (from *mānčaf, with m > w due to the labial f [see Cohen 1931:389-91], and with d > ṭ > č < *wānčaf > *wāčaf (with loss of w) > wānčaf (with insertion of n). Tna. wānčafa 'throw with a sling', Amh. wānčafčafa, are denomina- tives of wānčaf. The root passed into Cushitic: Sa. wānčif 'sling', Or. wačefo, Som. wadaf, Bil. wanšiba.

meaning: 'rain hard with gusts of wind'; wadf (K*) 'wind-driven rain'
'distress, make suffer, cause labor pains'; tawāddaḥa 'suffer, have labor pains'; tawdaḥt (D.TS) 'pain, labor pains, suffering, stress'
= Amh. , either anaffa 'bray', or annaffa 'blow on a fire'.
'plait, interweave'; wadin, wadinat (Lt) 'that which is plaited'

origin explanation: Dillmann 1376 (under tafāwāla) also quotes yäṭfāwālu from Fatha Nagaśt 11, 1 but Guidi's edition, p.94, has yäṭfāwwalu, for which see fwl.

phonetics: (yawfar, yufar, yafar) 'go to a field (for any agricultural activity), go out into the countryside, feed in a pasture', is extended to 'go any place';
meaning: 'go to a field (for any agricultural activity), go out into the countryside, feed in a pasture', is extended to 'go any place'; ʾawfara, causative; also 'take into a field, (Lt) send out, dispatch, make go forth, cause to browse'; tawāfara 'go outdoors to defecate (euphemism)'; wafāri, act. part. of wafarā; wafāre 'ad 'hewer of wood''; wafre 'countryside, field, farm, farmland, pas- ture, estate, husbandry'; (aṣada wafr 'field'); wafr 'husbandry'; wafār 'going to a field'; mofart (T) 'beam of a plough'; mofart (pl. mawāfər, mawāfərt) 'agricultural tool (spade, mattock)'; mufār 'farmland, pasture, village'
origin explanation: SEMITIC: Sar. wfr 'cultivate', m-wfr 'cultivated land belonging to a town'. Praetorius 1890:37 (so also Brockelmann 1908:595) connects it with Ar. nafara 'go on a trip or an expedition'. Conti 1978:116 connects it with a biradical root pr, but this root implies running, fleeing. See also Noeldeke 1910:186. ETHIOPIC: Tna. wāfarā 'go to a field', tāwaffarā 'plough, cultivate land'. For mofari 'agricultural tool', cp. Amh. mofār 'beam of plough'. Arg. mo- fāra, Gur. mofār 'wooden handle of a plough'; also in Cushitic: Sa. ifar 'go to a field'.
metathesis in relation to fws; see fawwasa.

meaning: 'burn (tr), ignite, cook'; wafāṭi (K*), act. part. of wafata; mafat, maft, moft, (T) mofat, mafit 'oven, fur- nace, pit for firing pottery';
origin explanation: (Gr.436 , with t, is undoubtedly to be corrected into wafata , with f); SEMITIC: Sar. wft 'burn'; this root is not to be compared with G. ʾafaya 'bake', as suggested by Dillmann 950 and Landberg 1909:1302. For Grébaut's wafata, see Rundgren 1961:367. ETHIOPIC: Tna. mofīṭi 'firing of pottery' (Coulbeaux- Schreiber 195), mafṣīṭi 'pit for firing' (da Bassano 134), from wft.

phonetics: (impf. yawaffi) 'receive, grant, transmit, consign, hand over, deliver, transfer, commit to someone's care, bequeath, entrust'
meaning: 'receive, grant, transmit, consign, hand over, deliver, transfer, commit to someone's care, bequeath, entrust'; tawaffayfa, tawaffayfa, pass. of awaffayfa; also 'receive, accept, take charge of'; wafuy, fem. wafit (Gr.468,K*), pass. part. of awaffayfa; also 'established, instituted'; wafayā (M) 'offer'; wafāyye 'yielding, tradition, (TS) gift'; wafyat 'accepting' (n); tawfīt, tawfīt 'tradition, (TS) gift, (T) receiving'; mofī (T) 'share, sharing'
origin explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. waffā 'pay a debt', Sar. wfy 'pay a debt, fulfill an obligation', h-wfy 'bestow', Soq wfe 'pay', Syr. ʾawfi 'bring to an end, achieve'. The connection with Heb. yāpe (h) 'pretty' (suggested by Buhl 309, Koehler 391, Baumgartner 404), and with Akk. (w)apū 'become evident' (von Soden 1459) is doubtful. ETHIOPIC: Tna. awāfäyä 'give, make a gift'. See also mawafa.
from Amh. wäg. For the Geez form, see waga II, below.
ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. wäg 'a rust affecting grain'.

phonetics: (yawgä, yagäb) 'pierce, prick, butt, gore, beat, hit, strike. (Y) make turbid, (T.Y) purify, refine (e.g. oil by beating)';
meaning: 'pierce, prick, butt, gore, beat, hit, strike. (Y) make turbid, (T.Y) purify, refine (e.g. oil by beating)'; note the opposite meanings 'make turbid, refine', both going back to the basic meaning 'beat'; in the one case 'make turbid by beating', and in the other case 'refine by beating'; tawagäʾa, passive; tawagäʾa 'pierce one another, butt one another'; wagāʾi, act. part. of wagga; wagu, pass. part. of wagga; wagʾat 'piercing, butting, stroke, (Lt) rheuma- tism'; mugāʾ 'stab (n), stroke'; mogəʾ, mogāʾ, (K) mugāʾ, (KBT) mogəʾ (pl. mawāgəʾ) 'mortar for pounding, ram (engine of war)'; mawgəʾ (K), same.
origin explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. wağaʾa 'hit with a knife or with the palm of the hand'. Soq wge 'hit', related to Sh. wg 'spoil something by making a hole in it', Heb. nāğaʿ 'gore' (with alternance n:w, for which see Noeldeke 1910:179-201). Brockelmann 1928:295 compares Syr. wgy (ʾafʿel) 'expel, remove'; cp. also Heb. wgy ygy (Buhl 287, Koehler 224). ETHIOPIC: Tna. wäggəʾe 'pierce'. Te. wägga, wäga, Amh.Arg.Gur. wägga. Note also Gur. aggäʾa 'pierce', Har. hağa from a related root wgy, hgy.
ETHIOPIC: Gur. wägga 'force to drink'; also Cushitic: Or. wägä 'make a baby drink'. See also gaʾata.

origin_explanation: awgā'a (Lt) 'follow the customs', wag' (K) 'narrative, tradition, custom, ceremonial'; wagā'āwi (K*) 'one who knows the customs, one who loves to talk'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. wāgī 'custom, story'. Amh. gur wāg 'tradition, custom'. Amh. (a)wāgga 'talk, discuss'. Te. (a)wāgā'a 'converse'. Amh. also wāggādā 'talk, converse', denominative from wāgāt 'conversation, talk'. See also wag.

meaning: 'arrive suddenly, attack suddenly, come upon suddenly, surprise, take unawares, (Y.TS) startle, frighten'
origin_explanation: gabi 'sudden occasion, unexpected opportunity, accidental event, recent event'; (ba-gabi; see gabta) 'accidental, casual, fortuitous, sudden'); (amālokta gabi 'new gods, recent gods', (mota gabi 'sudden death'); gabia 'suddenly, all of a sudden, unexpectedly, accidentally, instantly, recently'; gābtāwi 'sudden, unexpected'. SEMITIC: Ar. gibt 'idol' in Surah 4:51/54 is considered borrowed from G. gabi (Ludolf 531. Noeldeke 1910:47-8. Jeffery 99-100). However, gabi in Geez does not mean 'idol'. It was misunderstood as such because of the expression amālokta gabi 'idols of recent (time), recent gods'. On the various opinions concerning Ar. gibt expressed by Western scholars and Arab lexicographers, see Wahib Atallah, Arabi ca 17 (1970).71ff. On p. 81 he derives Ar. gibt from Qibt, a name given by the Arabs to the (ancient) Egyptians. See also tāw. ETHIOPIC: Te. awgāba 'take by surprise'. Amh. gābtāwi 'spontaneous' (from Geez).
awgada (K*), causative; ETHIOPIC: Tna. wāggādā 'retire, withdraw'. Te. awgāda 'hide'. Amh. wāggādā 'step aside'.

ETHIOPIC: Te. wāg bela 'shine, glitter', wāgwāg gim 'mer of a fire'. Tna. wāgāg bālā 'become day, dawn'. Amh. wāggāgā, wāggāg alā 'lighten, brighten'; perhaps related to wākayā, below. See also wāgāgān, below.
from Amh. wagagan; but perhaps rather from wagaga, above.

meaning: (K*) 'iron ring that holds the share of a plowbeam'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. wāgāl, same.

meaning: 'throw stone, cast, shoot, fire, (T) hit, strike'; wagara 'otāna 'fumigate, perfume with incense' (lit. 'throw incense'); wagara madfā'a 'discharge a cannon'; tawagāra, tawaggara 'be stoned'; tawāgara 'throw stones at one another'; wagāri, act. part. of wagara; (K*) 'fisherman (who throws the line)'; wagur, (Gr) wagər, pass. part. of wagara; wagər (pl. awgar, awgarāt) 'heap, mound, hill, mountain, high place (of worship), altar'; wagrat 'throwing, stoning, casting'; mawgar, mugār 'stone's throw, throw'; mogar 'fumigation'; (atāna mogar, type of a Geez poem consisting of nine couplets); mogari 'sling, catapult, (T) means of having something ascend'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. (early North Arabic) wgr 'tombstone' (W. Müller). A. Ambros suggests Ar. wagr 'Höhle, Berghöhle` < Steinformation < Stein'. For G. wgr 'heap, hill', cp. Aram. yagār 'hill, heap of stones'. Syr. yagrā 'heap pf stones', wgr, (śaf'el) śawgar 'hurl'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. Amh. wāggārā 'throw, strike with stones'. Te. wāgrā 'throw, drive away'. For 'hill', cp. Amh. wāgar 'hill' (probably from Geez), possibly to be derived from 'heap'.
from Amh. wāgāre, a variety of sorghum coming from the region of Wāgara.

meaning: (K*) 'a kind of perfume made by mixing aloes and perfume'
origin_explanation: from Amh. wagra.
wrongly for wa-grippā and grippā, above.

meaning: 'excommunicate, threaten with excommunication, anathematize, lay a ban, repudiate, banish, expel, separate, excise, curse'
origin_explanation: tawagza, passive; tawāgaza 'bind each other by imprecation or curse, alienate oneself from, turn away, avert'; waguz, pass. part. of awgaza; wagz, (Gr.468) wagz 'anathema'; wagzat 'excommunication, interdiction'; gaz, same; gəzat 'excommunication, anathema'. SEMITIC: Dillmann 939 derives wgz from a root gzz (gər, gzz, and others) 'cut, amputate'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. wāgəzā 'excommunicate'. Amh. wāgāzā, Gur. (a)wāggāzā; also Tna. gəzātā 'place under a ban or sanction'. Amh. gəzzātā, Gur. (tā)gəzzātā, denominatives from *gəzāt, from wgz. For the denominatives of this kind, see Muséon 75 (1961).159-164.
from Amh. wāgāz, same.

meaning: 'give, grant, confer upon, offer, bestow, consign, deliver, transmit, allow, permit'
origin_explanation: wahaba qāla 'utter, promise'; wahaba kidāna 'establish a covenant'; mannu yahubanni, mannu əmwahabanni 'would that!, May God give me!, I wish that!.'); hab, habi (fem.), habu (pl. masc.), habā (pl. fem.) 'let (me, us) come on!, now then, well!, here!'; 'awhabā, causative; tawhaba, passive; tawāhaba (K*) 'give each other'; 'astawāhaba 'repay, pay out'; wahābi 'one who gives, donor, generous'; wəhub, pass. part. of wahaba; wəhibo (T) 'gift'. habi 'gift, donation'; habhab 'gift, reward'; tawahbo 'gift'. SEMITIC: Ar. wahaba 'give', Sar. whb, Soq. weheb 'generous'. Heb. hab 'give!'. Aram. yəhab 'give'. Syr. ya(h)b, Md. yhb. ETHIOPIC: Tna. habā (from whb) 'give'. Te. haba. Gaf.Gur. wabā. Arg. hawa. Amh. wābi 'one who gives'; also in Cushitic: Bil. əw, Sa. Af. haw, Be. aū.
probably from Amh. waha 'water', wāhayyā 'become watery'.

metathesis in relation to wārh (note also h interchangeable with ḥ). However, it could also be identified with Semitic whr, yhr, as in Ar. wāḥir 'shining white' (Noeldeke 1910:189). Md. yur yura 'light, brilliance'. Heb. yāhir 'presumptuous' (Buhl 292).
tawḥāwaha 'be resplendent, shine, be bright, be cheerful, be glad'; wāḥāwē 'brightness, splendor, gladness'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. wawwa 'be tasty, be savory'; and perhaps Gaf. wāyā 'new'.
wāḥi ; see wahaya.

meaning: 'unite one thing to another (that is, 'make one'), add (in counting)'
origin_explanation: tawḥəda, tawāḥada 'be united, become one'; tawāḥada, (T) tawāḥada 'be united with each other, be one'; 'astawāḥada, 'astawāḥada, caus. of tawāḥada; also 'unite, declare to be one'; wāḥəd (fem. wāḥədt), (T) wāḥəd 'unique, only, one'; wāḥədāwi (Lt) 'sole'; wāḥədənna 'oneness, unity, union'; wāḥədənnat (Lt), same; tawāḥədo (t) 'oneness, unity, uniqueness, orthodoxy'; mawāḥəd, mastawāḥəd (K*) 'who unites'.

awgā'a (Lt) 'follow the customs', wag' (K) 'narrative, tradition, custom, ceremonial'; wagā'āwi (K*) 'one who knows the customs, one who loves to talk'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. wāgī 'custom, story'. Amh. gur wāg 'tradition, custom'. Amh. (a)wāgga 'talk, discuss'. Te. (a)wāgā'a 'converse'. Amh. also wāggādā 'talk, converse', denominative from wāgāt 'conversation, talk'. See also wag.

meaning: 'arrive suddenly, attack suddenly, come upon suddenly, surprise, take unawares, (Y.TS) startle, frighten'
origin_explanation: gabi 'sudden occasion, unexpected opportunity, accidental event, recent event'; (ba-gabi; see gabta) 'accidental, casual, fortuitous, sudden'); (amālokta gabi 'new gods, recent gods', (mota gabi 'sudden death'); gabia 'suddenly, all of a sudden, unexpectedly, accidentally, instantly, recently'; gābtāwi 'sudden, unexpected'. SEMITIC: Ar. gibt 'idol' in Surah 4:51/54 is considered borrowed from G. gabi (Ludolf 531. Noeldeke 1910:47-8. Jeffery 99-100). However, gabi in Geez does not mean 'idol'. It was misunderstood as such because of the expression amālokta gabi 'idols of recent (time), recent gods'. On the various opinions concerning Ar. gibt expressed by Western scholars and Arab lexicographers, see Wahib Atallah, Arabi ca 17 (1970).71ff. On p. 81 he derives Ar. gibt from Qibt, a name given by the Arabs to the (ancient) Egyptians. See also tāw. ETHIOPIC: Te. awgāba 'take by surprise'. Amh. gābtāwi 'spontaneous' (from Geez).
awgada (K*), causative; ETHIOPIC: Tna. wāggādā 'retire, withdraw'. Te. awgāda 'hide'. Amh. wāggādā 'step aside'.

ETHIOPIC: Te. wāg bela 'shine, glitter', wāgwāg gim 'mer of a fire'. Tna. wāgāg bālā 'become day, dawn'. Amh. wāggāgā, wāggāg alā 'lighten, brighten'; perhaps related to wākayā, below. See also wāgāgān, below.
from Amh. wagagan; but perhaps rather from wagaga, above.

meaning: (K*) 'iron ring that holds the share of a plowbeam'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. wāgāl, same.

meaning: 'throw stone, cast, shoot, fire, (T) hit, strike'; wagara 'otāna 'fumigate, perfume with incense' (lit. 'throw incense'); wagara madfā'a 'discharge a cannon'; tawagāra, tawaggara 'be stoned'; tawāgara 'throw stones at one another'; wagāri, act. part. of wagara; (K*) 'fisherman (who throws the line)'; wagur, (Gr) wagər, pass. part. of wagara; wagər (pl. awgar, awgarāt) 'heap, mound, hill, mountain, high place (of worship), altar'; wagrat 'throwing, stoning, casting'; mawgar, mugār 'stone's throw, throw'; mogar 'fumigation'; (atāna mogar, type of a Geez poem consisting of nine couplets); mogari 'sling, catapult, (T) means of having something ascend'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. (early North Arabic) wgr 'tombstone' (W. Müller). A. Ambros suggests Ar. wagr 'Höhle, Berghöhle` < Steinformation < Stein'. For G. wgr 'heap, hill', cp. Aram. yagār 'hill, heap of stones'. Syr. yagrā 'heap pf stones', wgr, (śaf'el) śawgar 'hurl'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. Amh. wāggārā 'throw, strike with stones'. Te. wāgrā 'throw, drive away'. For 'hill', cp. Amh. wāgar 'hill' (probably from Geez), possibly to be derived from 'heap'.
from Amh. wāgāre, a variety of sorghum coming from the region of Wāgara.

meaning: (K*) 'a kind of perfume made by mixing aloes and perfume'
origin_explanation: from Amh. wagra.
wrongly for wa-grippā and grippā, above.

meaning: 'excommunicate, threaten with excommunication, anathematize, lay a ban, repudiate, banish, expel, separate, excise, curse'
origin_explanation: tawagza, passive; tawāgaza 'bind each other by imprecation or curse, alienate oneself from, turn away, avert'; waguz, pass. part. of awgaza; wagz, (Gr.468) wagz 'anathema'; wagzat 'excommunication, interdiction'; gaz, same; gəzat 'excommunication, anathema'. SEMITIC: Dillmann 939 derives wgz from a root gzz (gər, gzz, and others) 'cut, amputate'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. wāgəzā 'excommunicate'. Amh. wāgāzā, Gur. (a)wāggāzā; also Tna. gəzātā 'place under a ban or sanction'. Amh. gəzzātā, Gur. (tā)gəzzātā, denominatives from *gəzāt, from wgz. For the denominatives of this kind, see Muséon 75 (1961).159-164.
from Amh. wāgāz, same.

meaning: 'give, grant, confer upon, offer, bestow, consign, deliver, transmit, allow, permit'
origin_explanation: wahaba qāla 'utter, promise'; wahaba kidāna 'establish a covenant'; mannu yahubanni, mannu əmwahabanni 'would that!, May God give me!, I wish that!.'); hab, habi (fem.), habu (pl. masc.), habā (pl. fem.) 'let (me, us) come on!, now then, well!, here!'; 'awhabā, causative; tawhaba, passive; tawāhaba (K*) 'give each other'; 'astawāhaba 'repay, pay out'; wahābi 'one who gives, donor, generous'; wəhub, pass. part. of wahaba; wəhibo (T) 'gift'. habi 'gift, donation'; habhab 'gift, reward'; tawahbo 'gift'. SEMITIC: Ar. wahaba 'give', Sar. whb, Soq. weheb 'generous'. Heb. hab 'give!'. Aram. yəhab 'give'. Syr. ya(h)b, Md. yhb. ETHIOPIC: Tna. habā (from whb) 'give'. Te. haba. Gaf.Gur. wabā. Arg. hawa. Amh. wābi 'one who gives'; also in Cushitic: Bil. əw, Sa. Af. haw, Be. aū.
probably from Amh. waha 'water', wāhayyā 'become watery'.

metathesis in relation to wārh (note also h interchangeable with ḥ). However, it could also be identified with Semitic whr, yhr, as in Ar. wāḥir 'shining white' (Noeldeke 1910:189). Md. yur yura 'light, brilliance'. Heb. yāhir 'presumptuous' (Buhl 292).
tawḥāwaha 'be resplendent, shine, be bright, be cheerful, be glad'; wāḥāwē 'brightness, splendor, gladness'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. wawwa 'be tasty, be savory'; and perhaps Gaf. wāyā 'new'.
wāḥi ; see wahaya.

meaning: 'unite one thing to another (that is, 'make one'), add (in counting)'
origin_explanation: tawḥəda, tawāḥada 'be united, become one'; tawāḥada, (T) tawāḥada 'be united with each other, be one'; 'astawāḥada, 'astawāḥada, caus. of tawāḥada; also 'unite, declare to be one'; wāḥəd (fem. wāḥədt), (T) wāḥəd 'unique, only, one'; wāḥədāwi (Lt) 'sole'; wāḥədənna 'oneness, unity, union'; wāḥədənnat (Lt), same; tawāḥədo (t) 'oneness, unity, uniqueness, orthodoxy'; mawāḥəd, mastawāḥəd (K*) 'who unites'.

SEMITIC: wḥd is connected with ḥd 'one' (see 'aḥadu'). Ar. waḥada, waḥuḍa 'be alone, be unique', waḥīd 'alone, single', Sar. k-wḥd 'in unison, together', Sb. (wḥd) ščḥad 'unite'. Heb. yāḥad 'be come united', yāḥīd 'only one'. Aram. iṭyaḥed 'be joined', Syr. iḥda 'only one, unique'. Ug. yḥd 'person without kin, an only son', Akk. wēdu 'only, alone, single'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. (tä)wähada 'become united' (from Geez), (a)wawada 'from *waddä'mix, combine'.

meaning: 'provoke to anger, irritate, stir up, agitate, revile, (K) importune'; wähaka (Lt) 'provoke, stir up'; tawähaka, tawahaka, passive; waḥāki, act. part. of wähaka; waḥakā (Lt) 'provocation'; waḥakat, waḥikat 'provocation, incitement'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: for Heb. ykḥ (bif-il hōkīḥ 'reprove') suggested by Ludolf 499, see rather wakkaha, below. The comparison with ḥkk 'scratch' (see ḥakaka, above), suggested by Dillmann 891, is unlikely.

meaning: (Lt) 'lend, make a gratuitous loan'; tawḥasa, tawahasa 'borrow, (M) guarantee'; tawāḥasa 'borrow, take a loan, (K*) borrow from one another'; 'astawāḥasa 'ask for a loan'; wāḥasī 'lender'; wāḥas (K*) 'lent'; wāḥs (D.Lt) 'loan, guaranty, security, surety, guarantor'; (nasrē wāḥs 'creditor'); wāḥsat 'lending, loan'; ḥasī (M) ḥasat 'loan, (Lt) guaranty'; tawāḥasī, mawḥasī, mawḥas 'lender'; tawāḥasī 'who receives a loan, who borrows'; tawḥast 'gratuitous lending'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Sar. (Qat) s-wḥs, 'give security'. ETHIOPIC: since taking a loan is connected with bringing a guarantor, the two notions are expressed by the same root: Te. wāḥasā 'take a guaranty, give security', tawāḥasā 'guarantee' (v.), wāḥasā 'guarantor'. Tna. (tä)wāḥasā 'guarantee', wāḥasā 'guarantor'. Amh. (a)wasā 'lend, bring a guarantor', täwasā 'borrow', was 'guarantor'. Har. wās, Gur. wasā 'lend (an object)', was 'guarantor, guarantee'; passed into Cushitic: Or. wāsi, Sid. wāse, Dar. wāse.

meaning: 'walk here and there, go back and forth'; wähwāhe (KG) 'going (n) here and there, visiting'; see waḥaya, below.

'walk about, wander, visit, frequent, inspect, watch over, be on the watch for, explore, lie in wait, (Lt) liberate, rescue'; wāḥaya (T) wāḥaya 'walk about, wander, visit, inspect, keep watch, be on the watch for, supervise, lie in wait, haunt, punish'; 'awāḥaya, 'awḥaya (K*), causative; tawāḥaya (Gr.465) 'visit frequently, (Gr) wander aimlessly, visit one another'; 'astawāḥaya, caus. of wāḥaya; also 'visit frequently'; wāḥāyi (M.K) wāḥāyi, act. part. of wāḥaya; wāḥuy (K*), pass. part. of wāḥaya, wāḥaya; wāḥāye (K) wāḥāyē (K) 'visiting, visit, watching over, inspecting'; wāḥāy (M) 'inspection, visit'; ḥayāt (K*), same as wāḥāy; mäwḥāy (DTW 761, under maḥay) 'place from which to keep watch, reason for visiting'; muḥayit, moḥayit (K*), same.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. waḥā (why, with ḥ) 'intend, have in mind'. Barth 1902:13 compares Ar. waḥā (why) 'hasten', Aram. why, (af'el) 'ōhī. For 'hasten', cp. also Sar. (Min) s-why 'accelerate', Sh. 'aha 'run to help', Ug. w/y/ḥy. ETHIOPIC: Amh. muḥay (DTW 761) 'place from which one keeps watch' (from Geez). Te. wāḥa 'determine the day of death (God)' (comes close to the meaning 'inspect, supervise'); see Littmann-Höfner 433. For 'walk about', see also wḥwh, above.

meaning: 'flow (stream, water), flow out, flow with (acc.), run down, pour down (tears)'; 'awḥaza 'shed, cause to flow, pour (tr.), pour out'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. wäḥazä 'flow'. Te. wäḥaza, Amh. wäḥz 'flowing' (from Geez). It is from the root wḥz that Praetorius 1879:28 derives Amh. wänz 'river' < *wāz (with inserted n) < wḥz; cp. also Gur. wänz 'river', Te. Tna. mäḥaz 'river', wäḥiz, wäḥig 'stream'. See also ḥayyazät.

meaning: 'be (become) few, be little, be small, diminish, be diminished, be inferior'; 'awḥada 'make few, make small, reduce to a small number, diminish, have something in a small quantity'; wäḥud, fem. wäḥadd (for wäḥadīt) 'small in number, a little bit, few in number, less, scanty'; (wäḥuda, ba-wäḥud 'a little, a little bit'); wäḥudān, wäḥudāt (pl.) 'few in number, some'; wäḥud (M) 'small, scanty'; wäḥd 'fewness'; wäḥdat 'scarcity, paucity, scantiness, small number, lesser amount'; 'awḥadot, same; mawḥadī, mawḥad (K*), act. part. of 'awḥada; 'awḥada.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. wäḥadä 'be little', Te. wäḥda, ḥada 'be little, diminish'.

meaning: 'gulp down, devour, swallow'; tawḥaṭa passive; tawḥaṭa (Gr.467) 'be devoured, be engulfed, devour one another'.

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: the connection with Ar. gawwaṭa 'swallow' (with metathesis, and ġ-ḥ), suggested by Dillmann 917 (followed by Praetorius, ZDMG 62[1908]:749), is unlikely. ETHIOPIC: Tna. wäḥaṭä 'swallow'. Te. wäḥaṭa, ḥaṭa. Amh.Gur. waṭa, Arg. wäḥaṭa; passed into Cushitic: Bil. waḥaṭ.

meaning: 'spring forth, shine forth, give light suddenly'.

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: perhaps related to Ar. waqā'a 'feel pain', Heb. yāgā 'be tired', Akk. egū 'tired'.

meaning: 'do something assiduously, be diligent, be curious'; 'awkaba (K*), causative; (T.Y) 'tire out, weaken'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. wakaba 'apply oneself perseveringly'. Sar. wkb 'be carried out (mission)'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. wakkābā 'be anxious to have something'. Te. wakābā 'urge to go away'.

meaning: 'take, accept, (T) put grain in a reservoir'; tawkafaka, tawakkäfa 'accept, receive, entertain (guests), admit, take up, be accepted, be received'; tawākäfa 'accept kindly, receive with deference, receive from one another'; 'awkafä (K*) 'cause to receive each other'; 'astawkafä (K*) 'cause to receive each other'; wäkuf 'accepting, being accepted, offering'; mukäf, (L) mäwkāf 'receptacle, place where one receives'; tawäkāfī, part. of tawkafä; tawäkāfī 'receiving (guests), reception'.

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: SAR. wkb 'receive, gain' (with alternance of labials; Biella 126). ETHIOPIC: Te. wākāfa 'receive', Tna. 'awākāfā 'give, hand over'. Amh. 'awākāfā 'give, hand to', tāwākāfī 'one who receives'.

meaning: 'clamor, shout, cry out', 'awkaka (K*) causative, tawākāha 'clamor, shout, strive one against the other, quarrel, provoke an altercation, dispute, argue', wakkh. (Lt) wak"h 'contention, quarreling, quarrel, strife, uproar, rivalry'; wakāh, same, wakkhat (Lt), same, tawākāhī 'obstinate, who stubbornly negates, who invites a quarrel';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: SAR. wkky-n 'altercation, dispute' (?) (Müller 1983-284), Heb. ykḥ (hi'fil) 'reprove, argue' (so also Ludolf 428, as against Noeldeke 1910:191, n.4); related to Ar. wakā'a 'reprove' (Guillaume I, 9); also Ar. wakwaka 'coo (pigeon)'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. (a)wākāka 'shout, raise a clamor', wakkaka 'commotion'; related to Te. (a)wka'e 'shout'. See also wakāta.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. wakkākā. same. See also 'awkaka.

meaning: (K*) 'become weak, give way, yield, be weary, be stunned, be frightened';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to Soq. 'ughe 'become reddish', perhaps Akk. akukūtu 'red glow in the sky'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. wakkā 'light' (from Geez). For its relation to wgg, see above.

meaning: 'confide, have confidence, trust, put trust, rely, have faith';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. takala 'trust in, rely', Aram. tkl, Syr. takel, Akk. takālu. For Heb. yākol 'be able, endure', see Buhl 299, Koehler 380. See also takala above. ETHIOPIC: Te. wakkāla 'entrust', tawakkāla 'trust', Tna. tāwakkālā, Amh. tāwakkālā 'trust in God'.

meaning: (M wrongly translates Tayyā's tāqabbālālā [rendering of tawakkara] as 'be received');
origin_explanation: Guidī 1906:919 considers it a misprint for tawakkafa 'receive', above. However, it is perhaps the same tawakkara that is translated by Gr.278 'be reconciled' and by D 1397 as 'reconcile'.

meaning: 'clamor, shout, cry out'; 'awkata, same; also caus. of wakata; tawākāta 'shout at one another, make a loud noise'; wakat (Lt) 'noise, clamor';
origin_explanation: it looks like a denominative of Amh. wakkāha 'clamor' (n.), for which see wakkāha, above.

meaning: 'shine, glisten, glitter, be brilliant, sparkle, flash, be resplendent'; 'awkāyā, (Y.M) 'awkaya, causative; also 'illumine something by its own light'; wākuy 'shining, bright, brilliant'; wakay, wakāy (Lt) 'sparkling stone'; wākā 'splendor, luster, brightness';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to Soq. 'ughe 'become reddish', perhaps Akk. akukūtu 'red glow in the sky'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. wakkā 'light' (from Geez). For its relation to wgg, see above.
from Amh. wala, of Cushitic origin: Qua. wālā. This root is perhaps common with Semitic w'l for which see warālā.
from Amh. wāllābā 'be shelled'.
SEMITIC: Ar. wālība 'offspring'; compared by von Soden 199 with Akk. elēpu 'send forth shoots'.

meaning: (T) 'take a gulp, hold in the cheeks'; walbat (Sw. in D 883, T.Gr.436) 'cheek, big lip';
origin_explanation: Dillmann 883 suggests a misprint for ḥalbāt 'cheek', but since Tayyā has also the verb (ta)walbaṭa, the reading walbaṭ seems to be correct.

meaning: 'give birth, beget, bear (a child), conceive, bring forth'; wallada (T.Y.M) 'choose, prefer'; OT 587 also translated walud by Amh. yātāmārrāṭu 'who were chosen'. However, if the translations given by T.Y.M. and OT are based on walud (waluda) 'aḥawāyā of Cant. 5:1 (as quoted in OT 587), the meanings 'choose, prefer' (T.Y.M.) and 'who were chosen' (OT) are not justified since waluda (walud) 'aḥawāyā means 'my young brothers'; see wald, below; 'awlada 'beget, cause to bear, deliver (a child), be a midwife'; tawalda, passive; also 'arise, come forth'; tawālada 'procreate, bear (children), increase by procreation, be multiplied with offspring, breed (intr.), be procreated, (Lt) adopt a child'; 'astawālada, caus. of tawālada; walādī (fem. walādīt, pl. walādayān, fem. walādāyāt) 'parent, procreator, begetter'; walādī 'fruitful, fecund'; walud 'born, begotten son, posterity'; also pl. of wald; see also wallada, above;
origin_explanation: (waluda beta krastiyān 'catechumen, one who receives elementary religious instruction'); (waluda ṭamqat 'baptized'); (waluda ḥaymānot 'faithful'); wald (pl. walud) 'son, child, boy, young one, disciple, servant'; also serves for the expression of age (e.g. kawino walda kal'e 'āmat 'he being two years old'); (wald qab) 'religious sect (see qab, above)'; (wald walud) 'grandson'; (waluda walud) 'grandchildren'; (waluda 'aḥuyā 'my young brother', or 'the son, my brother'; see S. Euringer, Biblica 17[1936]. 335ff); (waluda 'aḥ) 'walda 'aḥī 'nephew'; walatt (< walad-t), pl. 'awlādād 'daughter, girl, maidservant'; serves also for the expression of age (see wald, above).

meaning: (K*) 'sneak away from a task, slip away';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. walaqa 'penetrate into, sneak into'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. wāllāgā 'sneak away from a task'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. wālge 'jackal'.
see walwala.
Guidi 1906:920 derives it, with reservations, from Lat. vulpes 'fox'.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. wālāqā 'be dislocated', Amh. wāl- lāqa 'be taken off (clothes), be dislocated'. Arg. (a)wālāqa 'dislocate'. Gur. wālāqā 'slip off'; passed into Cushitic: wālāq 'be dislocated'.

meaning: (T has wrongly walqaya) = Amh. kāffala 'divide, pay'.
'who sings psalms, singer in a church chorus'
origin_explanation: corrupt from Gk. psaltēs 'who sings psalms'.

meaning: 'shield, (Lt) carrier of a shield, protection, arms, hand (special expression used in a guessing game; Dillmann 1357, under fasasa); waltawa (Y.MA) 'protect with a shield, ward off a blow with a shield' (denominative); 'awaltawa 'arm with a shield'; tawaltawa 'be protected with a shield, arm oneself with a shield, make a shield for oneself, (T) be in battle position'; waltawā 'arming with a shield or armor'; waltawē 'armed with a shield'; waltawāyi 'armed, equipped'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. walta 'round buckler', Tna. walta 'shield'. Amh. waltā 'a round wooden shield of sycamore, which is placed at the top of a central pillar'; from Cushitic: Kam. wolta, Dar. wonte, Or. wānta.
a denominative with the suffix -wa; see Stade 47.

meaning: 'change, exchange, alter, transform, transfigure, modify, substitute, reverse, barter, (Gr.272) transcribe, register'; tawallaṭa, passive; (zəyyəyəwəṭṭəllaṭ 'unchangeable, invariable'); tawallaṭa 'exchange with each other, acquire something by an exchange, be diverse'; 'astawallaṭa, caus. of tawallaṭa; wallaṭi (Gr.272), act. part. of wallaṭa; also 'who trades'; wəlluṭ, pass. part. of wallaṭa; also 'deviated, strange, diverse, different'; wallaṭē 'change, alteration, transformation, variation'; wallaṭoṭ 'change'; mawallaṭ 'one who changes, money changer, merchant'; tawallaṭi (Gr.272) 'changeable'; tawallaṭo(t) 'transformation, substitution, ex- change'; tawlāṭ 'exchange, barter, change, alteration, restitution, substitution, proxy, compensation, ransom, price, diverse things, contrary'; tawlāṭa, ba-tawlāṭ 'in exchange for'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. wallaṭā 'change, disguise oneself'; with metathesis in Tna.Amh. läwwäṭä 'change'. Gur. läwwäṭä; for Har. nā- wāṭa, see Leslau 1963:120.

meaning: 'doubt, hesitate'; 'awalwaḷa, 'awalāwalā 'perturb, consternate, agitate, cause to be undecided'; tawalwaḷa 'be agitated, be perturbed (in mind), be undecided, doubt, be hesitant'; tawalāwalā 'vacillate, be troubled (by doubt), doubt, hesitate, waver'; 'astawalwaḷa 'perplex, trouble, move hastily'; wəlwəl 'unstable, agitated, confused, perturbed'; wəlwəl 'perturbation, agitation, commotion, confusion, trepidation'; wəlwālē, (Lt) wəlwālē, same; 'awalwāli (K*), caus. of 'awalwaḷa; matwalwāl, (K) mawalwāl 'something that causes agitation or perturbation'; tawalwāli (Gr.465) 'who is agitated or perplexed'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. walwal 'anxiety, uneasiness'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. (tā)wālawāla 'hesitate, be undecided'. Te. wālawāla, Amh. wālawāla, wallāla. Gur. wālawāla, Har. wālala 'roam, walk about aimlessly'. The connection with Te. wālawāla 'blow (wind)' suggested by Littmann-Höfner 429, is possible. The root also occurs in Cushitic: Som. wilwil 'anxiety, worry'. See also Brockelmann 1950:33, and wlw, above.
wangelāwi 'evangelist, evangelical'. From Gk. euangelion; also in Te.Tna.Amh. wāngel. For the Geez origin of the Ar. 'injīl, see Noeldeke 1910:47, A. Fischer 1953:10.
perhaps from Amh. wanos, wane 'pigeon'. SEMITIC: the root comes close to Heb. yōnā 'dove'. Aram.-Syr. yawnā, Md. yawnā, Ug. yn-t. See also yōnās, below.

meaning: 'swim'; tawannaya 'play (singing and dancing), take pleasure (in)'; tawānaya 'play, amuse oneself, make merry, play together, take pleasure in, mock'; 'astawānaya, caus. of tawānaya; tawānayi 'player, actor'; tawnet 'game, play, toy, entertainment, making merry, music, folly, laughingstock, orgy, lust'; (makāna tawnet 'theater, place of exercise'); (nāwāya tawnet 'musical instrument'); tawānayo 'amusement'; mastawānī, (M) mastawānəy, (K*) 'astawānāyi 'actor, player'; mastəwnāy (Gr) 'theater, circus'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh.Gur. wannā (wny) 'swim', Arg. waṅ- na, Amh. tāwanay 'actor', tāwnet 'performance'.

meaning: 'strike, flay, skin, strip off, cut, (Gr.277) bruise, crush'; tawaqā (K*), passive; waqā'i (K*), act. part. of waqā; wəqu' (K*), pass. part. of waqā; muqā' (K*), moqā' (pl. muqā'āt, mawāqə') 'flail'.

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. waqa'a 'strike, hit, beat', Heb. yāqa' 'dislocate'; related to naq'a, above. ETHIOPIC: Te. wāqa 'beat, thresh', Tna. wāqe. Amh. Arg. wāqqa, Gur. wāq(q)a; related to wāqa. Note that Dillmann 913 separates this root into wq' I and wq' II.

'awqafa (T.MA) 'make a bracelet' (denominative); SEMITIC: Ar. waqf 'bracelet' is considered a Geez loanword (Noeldeke 1910:53). ETHIOPIC: Amh. wāqf 'thin piece of wood used to keep roof thatch in place'.

meaning: 'exca- vate, dig, hew, scrape, incise, hammer, quarry'; 'awqara, causative; also 'excavate, dig'; tawaqara, passive; waqāri, act. part. of waqara; (waqāre 'abn 'stonemason'); waqur, (Lt) wəqur 'scraped, hewn, quarried'; wəqro 'hewn work, quarried rock'; in a con- struct state situation it means 'dug, excavated', as in 'azqata wəqro 'excavated well'; waqrat (Gr.466) 'act of excavating, digging, quarrying'; moqar, moqart (pl. mawāqər) 'means of digging or hewing, chisel, drill'; mawqar (Gr.277), same.

D.Y 'reprimand, reproach, reprove, chide, call to account, accuse, condemn, defend, strive, quarrel, plead'; tawāqāsa, passive; tawāqāsa 'contend, contradict, dispute, argue a case, defend a case, make intercession, have a controversy, discuss, refute, plead, reprove, reject, bring to judgment, be brought to judgment, accuse each other, go to court'; 'astawāqāsa, caus. of tawāqāsa; also 'judge, accuse, cause to plead'; waqāšī, act. part. of waqāsa; waqūš (K*), pass. part. of waqāsa; waqāšā 'charge (in a lawsuit), defense'; qāšt. qāšāt 'dispute, strife, contention, controversy, litigation, plea, pleading, trial, judgment, judicial sentence, quarrel, suit, charge (in a lawsuit), vindication, defense'; muqāš 'court of justice, object of or reason for judgment'; mastawāqāš 'accuser'; tawāqāšī 'dispute'; waqāšāwi 'one who quarrels, quarrelsome, defender'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. waqāra 'break, split', SAr. wqr 'inscribed stone', m-wqrt 'rock boring', Heb. yāqar 'hew' (in I Kings 5:31, quoted from Barr 329). The root is related to nq'r, above; for verbs beginning with n:w, see Noeldeke 1910:184-5. Dillmann 912 (followed by Eitan 28) compares Ar. qāra (qwr) 'make a round hole', Heb. qār 'dig', cp. also Amh. q"ārāq"ārā 'make a hole in stone or in wood', Tna. q"ārāq"ārā 'be dug'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. wāgārā 'incise, chisel', Te.Arg. wāgārā, Amh. wāgārā, Har. wāgārā; passed into Cushitic: Sa. waqar, Kam. waqarro, waqara 'the stone used to sharpen the millstone'.

meaning: D.Y 'reprimand, reproach, reprove, chide, call to account, accuse, condemn, defend, strive, quarrel, plead'

(Gr.277) 'cut, cut asunder, cut off, cut away, divide, hew stones, slice, tear out, tear away, (T.L1) circumcise'; waqāqa, same; also 'twist, wring out a wet garment by twisting'; waqāqya, passive; waqāqy, pass. part. of waqāqa; waqəyat (K*) 'cutting, cutting asunder' (n); mawqāy (Gr.467); see moqāy; moqāy, moqāyit (pl. mawāqay), (Gr.467) moqāy 'instrument for cutting asunder or for tearing out, (Gr) knife'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. al-wāqī 'green woodpecker' (lit. 'one who cuts, hits'). ETHIOPIC: Amh. māwqəqe 'kind of disease', lit. 'that which cuts'. The root is related to waqā'a, above.

meaning: (D.T.Y.M) 'console, give comfort, watch over, look after'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. waqā (wqy) 'keep, preserve', Ug. yqy 'harbor, protection'; von Soden 1461 compares Akk. (w)aqū 'wait'. ETHIOPIC: Te. wāqāy 'strength, courage', Tna. 'awqāyā 'cause to prosper, put in good condition'; passed into Cushitic: Sa. waqay 'watch over'. Reinsch 1902:19 compares Som. ēg 'keep an eye on', Sa. ag, Be. ēg.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. wəqiyanos, Amh. wəqəyanos; for its Greek origin, see 'awqiyanos.

SEMITIC: in connection with Syr. 'ewqīṭā 'woman's kerchief, veil of silk and gold tissue' (for which see Payne Smith, supplement, p. 10), Ar. wiqāyā 'linge qu'une femme met sur la tête pour empêcher le voile d'être sali par la pommade' (from waqā 'protect').

from Lat. uncia through Gk. unkia > Aram. 'unqīyā > Ar. waqiyya; also in Te.Tna.Amh. waqet. For details on this unit, see Pankhurst 1970 1:59, 71. See also 'ənq"əyā. for 'awqāt, from Ar. 'awqāt; also in Te. waqāt, wākt 'time, season', Amh.Arg.Gur. wāqt, Har. wāqti.

meaning: 'support in old age' perhaps to be identified with war'a II.
'hide, cover' seems to be a reconstructed form from morā', which see mar'a above. See also wara I.

origin_explanation: metathesis of *war'a; see wa'ara.

meaning: 'spread grass on a floor scatter' the form warababa given by T.Y. is strange.

'descend, go down come down' 'awrəda 'make go down, lower, bring down take down, send down, lead down, lower'; tawārəda 'lower oneself, be deposed, be oppressed'; tawarrəda 'be possessed (by a spirit), be insane, be submitted'; tawārəda 'submit oneself, lower oneself, comply, possess (spirit possessing a person), be possessed (by a spirit), be insane, be agitated (by being possessed)'; 'astawārəda 'thrust down'; warādi, act. part. of warada; warud 'descending, deposed'; radat 'descent, going down, manner of going down'; 'awrādi (Gr), act. part. of 'awrəda; murād, məwrād 'place of descent, descent downward slope, cavity, valley'; (murāda 'aqab 'downward slope'); tawārədət 'submission'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. warada 'come to the water' (originally 'go down [to fetch water]'), cp. Amh. wārrədā 'descend, fetch water'), SAr. wrd 'descend', Soq. 'ered, Heb. yārad, Syr. yardā 'river' (the river being in a valley), Ph.Ug. yrd 'descend', Akk. (w)arādu. ETHIOPIC: Tna.Gur. wārādā 'descend, go down', Te. wārāda, Amh. wārrādā, Arg. wārrādā, Har. wārrādā; passed into Cushitic: Bil.Qua. wārād 'descend, go down'.

from Ar. ward; also Te. wərād 'dry petals of roses', Amh.Har. wārād 'rose'. As for Ar. ward, it is of Aram.-Syr. (wardā) origin (Salonen, SO 172 [1952]:2). Pbh. wəred is borrowed from Aramaic. For the Iranian origin of the root, see Salonen, op.cit. and Jeffery 287 (against Dietrich 296). Zimmern 55 considers Akk. amardīnu to be a possible origin of Aram. wardā.
from Ar. 'ard 'breadth, width'; also Tna. wārədī, Amh. wārədī; in Cushitic: Sa. warde. The derivation from Cushitic: Or. ballad 'width', suggested by Praetorius, ZDMG 47 (1893):393, is unlikely.
metathesis of marāwəḥt; see rwh I, and Amh. mārāwāt.
metathesis in relation to wāḥr.

SEMITIC: SAr. wrḥ 'moon', Sh. wrḥ, 'ərḥ 'fix a date', Mh. warḥ 'month', Heb. yāreḥ 'moon', Aram.Syr. yarḥā 'month', Md. iaḥra (with metathesis), Ph. yrḥ, Ug. yrḥ, Akk. (w)arḥu. Ar. 'arāḥa, warraḥa 'to date, write a history' is a denominative of tarīḥ 'date, history, chronicle' which seems to be of South Arabic (warḥ 'moon, month') origin (see F. Rosenthal, A History of Muslim Historiography, pp. 11-3). ETHIOPIC: Tna. wārḥi 'month', Te. wārəḥ, Har. wārḥi, Amh.Gur. wār, Arg. wārāḥ.
ETHIOPIC: Har. wārām 'spear', Amh. wārana; from Cushitic: Or. wārānā 'lance', Som. waran, pl. waramo, Aw. warəm.
SEMITIC: Ar. riq 'saliva', Heb. yārāq 'spit', Syr. raq (rqq).

SEMITIC: Derived from the root wrq 'green, yellow'. Ar. waraq 'leaves, foliage', SAr. wrq 'gold', Heb. yāroq 'green', Syr. Aram. yarqā 'herb', Md. Urg. Uq. yrq 'gold', Akk. arqu 'yellow, green'. It is from the meaning 'green' that the meaning 'vegetable, crop, leaf' developed in various Semitic languages. ETHIOPIC: Amh.Arg.Gur. wärq 'gold', Tna. wärqi, Te. wärq 'silver', Har. wärqi 'green' passed into Cushitic. Aw. worko 'gold, money', Bil. wärqā, Qua. warkā.

meaning: 'dissolute, one who does not observe the proprieties'.

'inherit, confiscate (governmental authority)'; 'awrasa 'cause to inherit, give an inheritance, bequeath, declare heir'; tawarsa 'be inherited, gain by inheritance, take for oneself by way of inheritance, receive an inheritance'; tawārasa 'inherit, get hold of, divide an inheritance with others, take possession of'; 'astawārasa, caus. of tawārasa; also 'distribute an inheritance'; warāsi 'heir, successor'; wārāšī 'heir, inheritor'; warsat 'inheritance'; rast 'inheritance, heritage, estate, property, succession'; 'awrāsī 'one who makes inherit, testator'; moras (pl. mawāras, mawārəsť) 'heir, inheritor, co-heir, inheritance'; tawārāšī 'coheir'; tawārāso 'succession'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. warita 'inherit', SAr. wrt. Soq. 'eret. Heb. yāraš, Aram. yəret. Syr. 'ret. Ug. yrt. For 'inherit, confiscate', cp. Heb. yāraš 'inherit, seize' (Dahood 1970:63).

ETHIOPIC: Tna.Gur. wäräsa 'inherit', Te. wärsa, Amh.Gaf. wärräsa 'inherit, confiscate', Arg. wärräsa, Har. wäräsa; note the Tna. denominative tärästäyä 'inherit' from rəsti. The root passed into Cushitic: Aw. wores-x'a, Bil. wäräs, Af. waras.
the singular is probably reconstructed from the pl. 'awrās, for which see 'awrās, above.

meaning: 'throw, throw away, cast off, cast down, cast forth'; tawarra, tawarrawa, passive; also 'rush forward, throw oneself'; warāwī, act. part. of warawa; waraw, pl. warawāt (Gr.275) 'javelin, dart'; warzəwə (fem. warut) 'thrown'; wərwat (Lt) 'throwing' (n.).
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. warra (wrr) 'throw', SAr. wrw 'attack', Heb. yārā (yry) 'shoot', Ug. yrw. Delitzsch 41 compares Akk. arū 'vomit'. The connection with Akk. warū 'lead, guide', suggested by von Soden 1473, is unlikely. ETHIOPIC: Te. wärära 'throw', Tna. wärärä. Amh. wärräwärä, Arg. wäräwwärä, Gur. worawärä. See also wrwr.
is considered by Guidi 1906:919 a misprint for marwad, maräwäd, from Ar. marāwīd 'dropper for collyrium'. See also maräwäd, above.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. wäräwärə 'gem, blue color' (from Geez).
from Amh. wärräwärä 'throw'. See also warawa.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. wära 'announce', wärä 'communica tion', Tna. wärə 'notice, fame', Amh. wärä 'news'.

meaning: 'become a young man, mature, act courageously'; 'awarzawa 'rear to adulthood, bring up a young man'; tawarzawa 'become vigorous like a young man'; warzəw (fem. warzəwt, warzət) 'adult'; with suffix pronouns; e.g. warzəwaka 'you being an adult'; warzəwī (pl. warzəw, warzəwət) 'young, young man, youth, adolescent, brave, young (also referring to animals; e.g. warzəwä hayyal 'young stag')'; warzəwī 'young' (adj.); wərzäwə 'youth, manhood, maturity, courage, valor, prowess'; warzət 'youth, adolescence, manhood, maturity'; warzəwənnä, same.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Praetorius 1890:373 identifies it with the root wrs 'inherit', but there is no cogent reason why the voiceless s of wrs should become z in warzəwā 'young man'. Likewise the connection with Ar. wazā (wzy) 'be collected, be heaped up' (with augmented r), suggested by Stade 25, is doubtful, as is the connection with Amh. wärsa 'brother-in-law' suggested by Wajnberg 1935:19. The connection with Šh. aršot 'children, boys' (from warsot, root wrš), suggested by Bittner 1916:51, with reserva tions, is likewise unlikely. ETHIOPIC: Tna. wärəza 'young man', wärzəwä 'be mature', Te. wärza 'be mature', wərza 'youth'. Amh. wärəza (probably from Geez), Gur. qärəza 'young man in his prime', possibly from q'rəz be coming wrz, or from wrz becoming q'rz by an inverse process. For a similar occurrence, cp. Amh. qäzäbbärä and qäzäbbärä 'rave' (cited in Cohen 1939:47).

meaning: 'take a wife, marry (man or woman)'; tawasba 'be married'; tawāsaba 'intermarry, get married'; 'astawāsaba, 'astawsaba 'give in marriage, marry off, arrange a marriage'; wasub 'married'; wasubān (pl.) 'married couple'.
origin_explanation: from *wsb 'sit' (see 'Semitic', below). 'awsaba 'make sit, make dwell (the bride in the bridegroom's house)'; cp. Amh. agäbba 'marry', lit. 'make enter (the bride into the bridegroom's house)', and Syr.Md. below. SEMITIC: Ar. (Had) ta-wattab 'sit in the oriental manner', SAr. wtb 'sit, reside', Heb. yāšab 'sit', Aram. yaṭeb. Syr. iṭṭeb 'sit', 'awṭeb 'marry', Md. yṭb 'sit', ('af-el) auṭib 'marry', Ug. yṭb 'sit', Ph. yšb, Akk. wašābu. See on this root Marrassigni 16-18. Note also Ar. wataba 'jump', in Rabin, FRCS 1 (1970). 281-3. ETHIOPIC: Amh. (a)wāššäbä 'copulate' (from Geez). Tna. 'awāššäbä 'marry', take a spouse'; passed into Cushitic: Sa. wasibō 'marriage tie between two families'. The meaning of G. 'awšaba 'marry' is considered by Rabin, ib., p. 282 a calque from Syriac.

meaning: 'take, lead, lead away, carry away, carry forth, conduct, bring'; tawasda (K*), passive; mawsəd (K*), musəd (M) 'means of taking, or leading, and so on'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: It is tempting to connect it with ysd, wsd 'lay a foundation' (Heb. yāsad, Ug. ysd), in case the basic meaning of G. wasada is 'lead', the leading or going first would then be comparable to the laying of a foundation, that is, the first part of a building. ETHIOPIC: Tna.Gur. wäsädä 'take', Amh. wässäda.

meaning: 'add, join to, augment, supplement, increase'; 'awassaka (Lt), causative; tawassaka, passive; also 'increase' (intr); wassuk, pass. part. of wassaka; wassäkä 'addition, increment, increase, extra amount'; wassäkoi 'increase, increment'; tawšäk, (T) tawšäk 'addition, additional amount, supplement, increase, increment, accu mulation, appendix'; (wa-tawšäkä 'and more').
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna.Gur. wässäkā 'add, increase', Te. wäsäka, Amh. wässäkä, Har. wäsäka, Arg. wässäkā, Gur. wässäkä, Slt. wässäkā.
SEMITIC: Perhaps Akk. esēku 'assign, allot'; related to Ar. wasaqa 'store, heap up'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. wäsäkä 'add', Te. wäsäka, Amh. wässäkā.

meaning: 'pass a ring or a cord through the nose (in order to control a large ox), rule with an iron hand'
origin_explanation: waskamt (T.M) 'ring or cord put through the nose (of a large ox), reins for checking a mount, (Lt) yoke'; from Amh. wäskämt 'ring put through an ox's nose'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. wasma, wäsma 'thornbush'.

meaning: 'delimit, determine, mark off, define, fix (limits), establish'
origin_explanation: 'awassana (K*) causative; tawassana, passive; (zayyəyətwessan 'illimitable'); tawäsana 'border upon, have a common border, be contiguous'; 'astawäsana (K*), caus. of tawäsana; wassāni, act. part. of wassana; wassun, pass. part. of wassana; also 'predetermined'; wasan, (L) wasn 'boundary, limit, border'; wässänə 'delimitation, definition, prescription, regulation, decision, term, limit'. SEMITIC: Ar. (Yem) watan 'stone delimiting the border', Sar. wtn 'delimit, set bounds'; not to be connected with Ar. wasama 'mark', as suggested by Dillmann 904 and Brockelmann 1908:154. ETHIOPIC: Tna. wässänä 'delimit a boundary', Amh. wässänä, Arg. wässänä, Gur. wässänä, 'limit'; Tna.Amh.Arg.Gur. wäsän; passed into Cushitic: Or. wesana 'delimit', Kam. wässanno.

meaning: 'bend (a bow), shoot (from a bow), (Y) stretch, pull, straighten up'
origin_explanation: tawasaqa, passive; wasuq 'bent (bow), strung (bow)'; ETHIOPIC: Littmann-Höfner 438 compare it with Te. wäsäqa 'tie up in a net'.

meaning: 'in, into, upon, on to, at, toward, among'
origin_explanation: ba-wasta 'within, in, at, on, among'; 'əm-wasta 'out of, from, down from, from within'; 'ənta wasta 'at, through'; with suffix pronouns the base is wastət-; is a variant of wəst, below.

(za-wəst 'internal'); (ba-wəst 'within'); ('əm-wəst 'from within'); ('ənta wəst 'within, inwardly'); wəsta (adv.) 'within, in, inwardly'; wəsta, wəstə 'on the inside, inwardly'; wəsṭāti, wəsṭātī 'inner, intimate, secret'; wəsṭātīt, wəsṭātīt (pl. wəsṭātəyāt) 'inside, what is inward, inner apartment, inner chamber, secret chamber, inner portion'; wəsṭəy, see wəsṭā; wəsṭātāwi, wəsṭātāy (pl. wəsṭātāwəyāt) 'inner, innermost part'; wəsṭāte (pl. wəsṭātəyāt) 'interior, inside, middle, innermost, recess'. SEMITIC: Ar. wast, wasat 'middle', SAr. wsṭ. Brockelmann 1913:419 compares Akk. ištu, ustu, ultu, for which see von Soden 1952:165. See also Rundgren 1955:177 and Kienast, Orientalia 26 (1957), 261. Torczyner, ZDMG 66 (1912), 768 compares PBh. wešēṭ 'gullet' > "*middle of the body"; see also Rabin, El 9 (1969),149. ETHIOPIC: Te. wast 'inside' (n), wəsṭāte 'interior', Tna. wäsṭə 'inside', Amh. Gur. wəst, Har. ustu; for Arg. wəfē, see Leslau 1979:668.
From Ar. wisād (pl. of wisād).

meaning: 'answer, respond, respond in chant, speak, hearken' (that is, respond to a prayer)
origin_explanation: tawāšə'a, tawāšə'a 'discuss, dispute, argue, quarrel, contend, speak against, contradict, refute, conduct a learned controversy, defend a case';

'astawäsə'a (Lt) 'prepare a legal case'; 'awšə'a, part. of 'awšə'a; 'awšə'o (t) 'answer' (n); tawāšə'a, part. of tawāšə'a; tawāšə'ot 'debate, speech, reply, dispute, argument, contrariness'; mawšə'a, mawšə'i (Lt) 'who contradicts'; mošā (pl. mawšə't) 'antiphonal chant, response'; for the composition of the mawšə't 'antiphonal chants', see Getatchew 1979 no. 1206, 18, Velat 60; for a bibliography on the mawšə't, see Hammerschmidt-Six 1983: 103; šāšə'a, šāšə'a, šəšəw, see above. SEMITIC: SAr. (h)wsy (with c) 'grant favor to' (Müller 1983:284). Barth 1893:63-4 suggests Hebrew root *wsy in maśśā (') 'utterance' or in the expression lō tiśśā (') (Ex. 20:7) 'du sollst nicht aussprechen'. Praetorius 1890:37 connects it with G. naś'a 'raise (the voice)', Heb. nāśā(')-, same, with alternance n:w; so also Brockelmann 1908:595, and see Noeldeke 1910: 193-4. ETHIOPIC: Tna. 'awšə'e 'bring to mind, refer to', täwasə'e 'ask one another', Amh. (a)wässa 'talk about something'. Reinisch 1890:373 connects it with Cushitic: Sa. waza 'express an opinion'.

wašara (yawšər, yašər, yašär), waššara (L.T) wasara 'saw, cut with a saw, split with a saw'; tawšara, tawšasara, passive; tawšasara 'use the saw with another, saw (two or more people)'; wašāri 'one who saws'; wəšur 'sawed'; waššāre (K*) 'sawing (n.), being sawed'; mošar, mošart (pl. mawšar, mawšart) 'saw'; maššār, (L) maššār 'saw, axe, hatchet'. SEMITIC: Ar. wašara 'saw', Mh. wušur 'build'; related to 'ašara, našara 'saw' (v); PBh. nāšar, Heb. nāšār 'saw' (n.), Aram. maššārā, Syr. maššārā, Akk. maššāru, from *nsr, Aram.-Syr. nəšar 'saw' (v.), Akk. ašāru, Ug. ššr-t 'saw' (n., from šršr, Caquot 247), Akk. šaššāru (from šaršāru). For n:w, see Noeldeke 1910:182, Landberg 1942:277. ETHIOPIC: Te. šäršära 'saw', Tna. šäršära, Amh. šäršära. For G. mošar 'saw', maššār 'axe', cp. Tna.Amh. maššar 'axe', Te. mäšar; perhaps Gur.
The root passed into Cushitic: Sa. mišär 'axe', Be. meśär.

tawassa'a, tawassə'a 'lose a battle, be defeated, be overcome, succumb'; wasā'i, mawasə'a (K*), act. part. of wasaqa; tawasaqa (Lt) 'suffer tediousness, weariness, or restlessness'; Amh. wäṭṭa 'win a lottery' is probably to be identified with wäṭṭa 'go out', from wəṭ, wəṣ.

meaning: 'nose or ear ornament, earring, nose ring'
origin_explanation: 'awšābāt 'adornments'; wašaba (MA.K* Sw. in D 943), 'awšaba (T) 'work (metal, rings)', denominative.
probably a misprint for wəqafa 'work', same meaning (see wqf). wəsf, see mawšaft.

tawasha (Lt) 'suffer tediousness, weariness, or restlessness'; wəsuh (Lt) 'who is restless, that is tedious'; tawəšəh (Lt) 'tediousness, weariness, restlessness, lightness, thoughtlessness'; ṭuṣəh (Lt), same; mastawəšəh (Lt) 'wearisome, tedious, thoughtless'.

'awšaha 'pour out, let fall in drops, offer libation'; tawasha (K*), pass. of washa, 'awšaha; tawashə'a 'mingle, associate (lit. 'one thing being poured into another'), (T) be seized'; mošəh (K*), mošəht (pl. mawšəh, mawšəht) 'drink-offering, pouring vessel, container used for libation (bowl, saucer), (KG) = Amh. sǝls 'kind of sauce'; šəhšəh 'leakage, dripping moisture'; mošha, see above.
ETHIOPIC: related to Tna. wāṣwāṣā 'shake, jolt, move'. Te. wāṣwāṣa, Amh. wāṣwāṣā.

From Ar. waṭan 'idol'. As for Ar. waṭan, it is considered by Fraenkel 273 (followed by Jeffery 286-7) to be taken from SAr. wtn; so also W. Müller, 'Grenzstein > Grenzidol > Götzenbild (aus Stein)'. J. Wellhausen, Reste arabischen Heidentums (1897), p.102 states that Ar. waṭan is not Arabic in origin, but he does not specify its origin.

meaning: 'do something with atten-tion, direct attention to, pursue earnestly, fix (the eyes upon), set (the eyes upon), persevere, continue, be assiduous';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. waṭara 'stretch a bow'. For 'sinew, tendon, cord', cp. Ar. waṭar, Heb. yeṭer, Aram.-Syr. yaṭrā 'string of bow', Md. yaṭra 'tendon, bow-string'. ETHIOPIC: Te. wāṭāra 'stretch a bow, stretch by pulling, pull tight', Tna. Amh. wāṭṭārā, wāṭṭārā, Arg. weṭṭāra, Gaf. wāṭṭārā, Gur. wāṭārā, passed into Cushitic: Qua.Bil. wāṭār 'stretch'. For 'sinew, cord', cp. Te. wāṭār 'tune of a harp' (expanded from 'string of a harp'), Tna. wāṭār 'chant accompanied by the harp', Amh. wāṭār 'bowstring'. Har.Gur. wāṭār 'nerve, sinew, tendon'.

meaning: 'bend, stretch tight, tighten (strings), straighten up (probably a de-nominative from waṭr), (Y) prick the ears (don-key)';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. waṭara 'stretch a bow'. For 'sinew, tendon, cord', cp. Ar. waṭar, Heb. yeṭer, Aram.-Syr. yaṭrā 'string of bow', Md. yaṭra 'tendon, bow-string'. ETHIOPIC: Te. wāṭāra 'stretch a bow, stretch by pulling, pull tight', Tna. Amh. wāṭṭārā, wāṭṭārā, Arg. weṭṭāra, Gaf. wāṭṭārā, Gur. wāṭārā, passed into Cushitic: Qua.Bil. wāṭār 'stretch'. For 'sinew, cord', cp. Te. wāṭār 'tune of a harp' (expanded from 'string of a harp'), Tna. wāṭār 'chant accompanied by the harp', Amh. wāṭār 'bowstring'. Har.Gur. wāṭār 'nerve, sinew, tendon'.
perhaps taken from Amh. wāṭāt 'milk'.

meaning: 'heap, pile up, arrange in layers, put in order, spread';
'crowd around (cattle)', coming close to the Geez meaning 'heap, pile up'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. wṭḥ, tawāṭāḥa 'crowd around with (cattle)', coming close to the Geez meaning 'heap, pile up'. ETHIOPIC: Te. wāṭḥa 'pile up', Amh. moṭaḥi 'priest's clothes' (from Geez), perhaps also Gur. m°ṭ°ḥ 'clothes, dress' (with ṭ < ḥ). The connection with G. ʿalṭāḥ 'kind of tunic' (see above), suggested by Rabin, SH 8 (1961). 392, is unlikely. See also moṭṭoḥa.
ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh.Gur. wāṭṭānā 'begin'; con-nected with Te. wāṭṭānā 'try, test' (that is, 'begin something'), Gaf. wāṭṭānā 'taste'; passed into Cushit-ic: Bil. waṭan 'try'.
SEMITIC: Ar. waṭis 'furnace'.

meaning: 'clamor, raise a shout, shout loudly, cry aloud, howl, roar, wail';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. wawwaʿa 'shout' (v.), wawwaʿ 'barking', wawʿā 'clamor'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. waw wāʿā 'cry, lament', Te. wāwā, wāʿa 'cry', hawā 'howl', Amh. wa wa, wawa 'sound of a crow, aw, sound of a hyena, Gur. wawaʿ 'crow'.

meaning: 'calamity, misfortune, woe, wail-ing'; from (see above) + l°to';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. wayl 'woe!', waḥwala 'lament', Soq. waylo 'woe (to)', Heb. yll (hifʿil) 'utter a howl, lament', Aram. yəlel 'lament', Syr. (ʿa)yəlel. ETHIOPIC: Tna. wāylāy 'cry of woe', wālāl 'cry of lamentation', Amh. wāyyəloh 'woe to you!', wāylāy 'cry of woe', wālālē 'cry of distress', Har. wāyli 'woe!'.

SEMITIC: Ar. wāyn 'vineyard', Heb. yayin 'wine', Ug. yn, Akk. īnu (from Canaanite). For Ar. wayn 'black (or white) grapes', see Rabin, Orientalia 32 (1963). 138, n.8. On the origin of wayn from Asia Minor, see Landberg 1942:2944. On the Hit-tite (*wiyana-) origin, see Fronzaroli, AG141 (1956). 37 and Rabin, ib. See also Buck 390. ETHIOPIC: Tna. wāyni 'wine, grape', Amh. wāyn 'grape', passed into Cushitic: Be. wayni.

meaning: 'kind of wild she-goat, roe, kind of gazelle';
origin_explanation: from Cushitic: Ar. wayṭale; also in Har. waydali 'gazelle', Amh. wāyṭal. It is not to be connected with Ar. tayṭal, as suggested by Hommel 286.

ETHIOPIC: Tna. wāzāfā, wānzāfā 'leave something undone or unfinished', Amh. wāzzāfā; Amh. also zāfāzzāfā 'leave unfinished' < wzf with loss of w and reduplication of zf; passed into Cushitic: Qua. wāzzāf y.

meaning: 'decorate, give extra touches, (KG) dress in a licentious manner'; tawazfa, same; təwzafti (T.B.M) 'ornament, having (n) a high opinion of one's self';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Schwally, ZDMG 52 (1898). 133 compares it with Ar. zāfa (zyf) 'walk proudly'. ETHIOPIC: Te. (ta)wānzāfā 'wrap oneself up, put on one's clothes', Tna. tāwānzāfā, Amh. wānāzzāfā 'wear a šämma-garment with the hem's border stripe passing over either shoulder'; note the root augmented by n. See also zwf, təzwəft.

meaning: 'eve (of a holiday or festival), vigil of a holiday, the whole day before a festival, chants of a vigil, hymns sung on the eve of a holiday' (see Velat 297ff); wāzəmā mahātəw 'hymns sung on the eve of a holiday';
The analysis of wāzəmā as coming from wāy zemā 'oh zemā' (see zemā, below), suggested by Euringer, ZS 10 (1935). 135, is doubtful.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. wazema. The analysis of wāzəmā as coming from wāy zemā 'oh zemā' (see zemā, below), suggested by Euringer, ZS 10 (1935). 135, is doubtful.
probably from Ar. wazara 'fill up a fissure'; also Amh. wāzzārā 'decorate, adorn'.

meaning: 'vizier, minister of state'; 'astawzara 'appoint a vizier'; denominative;
origin_explanation: from Ar. wazīr; also Amh. wāzīr, wazīr 'chief adviser or of a monarch'.
from Amh. wāyzāro.

SEMITIC: related to Ar. hazhaza 'shake, quiver', Šh. hezz, ehezhez. ETHIOPIC: Te. wāzwāza 'jolt, shake', Tna. wāzwāzā 'move, shake', Amh. wāzāwwāzā 'shake, agitate', tāwāzāwwāzā 'swing back and forth'; passed into Cushitic: Qem. wāzāwāz 'move continually'. See also wzz.
related to wazwaza, above.
